# Security Known Limitations

**Generated by:** Murderbot security review, 2026-05-30 (T-murderbot-security-review)
**Threat model:** Agents are prompt-injectable but not inherently malicious. Rack operators are trusted. ContainerShim tier agents are explicitly untrusted. Adversary capability: manipulate an existing trusted agent via prompt injection; does NOT include physical machine access or separate-process network attacks.

This document records gaps that are acceptable v1 risks or explicitly deferred, so future readers know they were considered — not overlooked. HIGH-severity gaps with in-scope fix paths are ticketed separately.

---

## Ticketed gaps (fix in progress)

These were found during the review and have been filed as fix tickets. They are not listed here as "acceptable" — they are active work items.

| Ticket | Finding |
|---|---|
| T-halt-caller-auth-token | `from_device` on halt/block/resume is a self-reported string — any prompt-injected agent can pass `from_device='skeleton'` to halt any device or resume itself after halt |
| T-provenance-write-enforce | `source_token` stored without verification in cortex.py — a manipulated agent can write memories with a fabricated token, poisoning trust-tier scores |
| T-announce-proof-validation | Broker issues a token to any caller claiming a recognized `agent_id` — the `proof` field is stored but never validated; identity spoofing by a ContainerShim-tier agent is trivially possible |
| T-container-shim-network-spec | ContainerShim spec says "loopback socket to the shim only" without specifying whether a bridge network creates a host-reachability path; must be pinned before implementation |

---

## Acceptable risks (known limitations)

### KL-1: HMAC shared secret, not PKI

Tokens use HMAC-SHA256 with a shared rack secret. Cross-rack federation, token delegation, and cryptographic non-repudiation require asymmetric keys (PKI). These are explicitly out of scope for v1 (see T-provenance-identity scope boundary). Acceptable because the rack is a single-machine trust domain.

### KL-2: Token expiry is session-scoped, not time-scoped

Tokens expire on agent deregister or rack restart, not by clock. A prompt-injected agent that is registered indefinitely retains a valid token. Time-based expiry would require clock synchronization and a background reaper. Acceptable for v1 given session-scoped TTL is already short in practice.

### KL-3: PII detection is regex-based and bypassable

The output validator (T-shim-output-validation) uses regex patterns for email/phone/SSN. Unicode homoglyphs, base64 encoding, or spacing tricks can evade detection. Semantic content validation is explicitly out of scope for this phase — assigned to the evaluator device (not yet ticketed). The current regex layer catches accidental leakage, not adversarial evasion.

**Security-critical path note:** Prompt injection via tool results (e.g., "ignore previous instructions" in a tool response) is the primary stated threat and has **no output-layer defense** today. The evaluator device ticket, when filed, should be tagged security-critical.

### KL-4: Output validation is non-blocking by default

Per T-shim-output-validation design, failed PII validation redacts and logs but does not block delivery. For ContainerShim-tier (untrusted) agents, this means PII can still pass through. Explicitly by design for v1; blocking default is a follow-on configuration option.

### KL-5: bus inter-agent communication bypasses policy gate

The policy gate (T-shim-policy-gate) enforces tool calls routed through BaseShim.dispatch(). Direct bus message exchange between agents is not subject to the gate. This is an intentional architectural gap: content-layer message inspection would require deep interception at the bus layer. Acceptable for v1 given all agents are same-UID same-machine trusted processes.

### KL-6: `source_agent` in memory_writer.py is self-reported

The Librarian write endpoint (`memory_writer.write_memory`) accepts `source_agent` as a string parameter with no token backing. This is the denormalized copy for query speed (T-provenance-write-attribution design). Attribution integrity for Librarian-path writes depends on the caller being honest. Acceptable because Librarian is a trusted-tier device; full token verification is being enforced on the cortex.py path (T-provenance-write-enforce).

### KL-7: Policy YAML has no integrity protection

Policy rules in `config/policies/` are YAML files with no signature check. An attacker with filesystem write access could inject permissive policies. Mitigated by: filesystem access = already-compromised host; YAML is loaded read-only; operator can detect changes via git diff. Acceptable because filesystem compromise is out of the threat model.

### KL-8: halt/block access control uses localhost trust (v1)

Existing `{device_id}_halt` and `{device_id}_block` MCP tools use a self-reported `from_device` string for authorization ("localhost trust; crypto ACL Phase 5+" per skeleton.py). This is acknowledged technical debt in the code comments, not an undiscovered gap. The kill-switch design (T-agent-kill-switch) will add the generalized halt/resume tools — T-halt-caller-auth-token ensures they use token-backed identity before shipping.

### KL-9: Trust tier gaming follows from identity (will be fixed by T-announce-proof-validation)

Trust tier derivation (T-provenance-trust-scoring) is only as strong as identity issuance. If a ContainerShim agent can claim `agent_id='igor'` and receive a tier_2 token, all its writes appear as tier_2. This is not a separate gap — it's a consequence of T-announce-proof-validation being open. Will close when proof validation ships.

### KL-10: Docker socket in ContainerShim (operational checklist)

The ContainerShim spec does not explicitly forbid mounting the host Docker socket (`/var/run/docker.sock`). If `allowed_mounts` in a profile included the Docker socket, container escape via the Docker API becomes trivial. This should be enforced as an explicit deny in ContainerShim.start() regardless of the profile, and documented in the profile spec. Tracked as part of T-container-shim-network-spec.

---

## Reviewed tickets — summary

| Ticket | Finding | Disposition |
|---|---|---|
| T-provenance-identity | Token forgery requires rack.secret access; same-UID process can always read it — in-model attacker is a prompt-injected agent reading the file via a tool call | Ticketed: T-announce-proof-validation (identity spoofing), T-halt-caller-auth-token (token-backed auth) |
| T-provenance-write-attribution | source_token stored without verification | Ticketed: T-provenance-write-enforce |
| T-provenance-trust-scoring | Trust tier gameable via identity spoofing | Follows from T-announce-proof-validation |
| T-shim-policy-gate | dispatch() is advisory (direct calls bypass gate); cold-start window; bus bypass | KL-5; direct-call bypass is code-smell requiring discipline, not a runtime attack surface |
| T-shim-output-validation | Regex PII evasion; no prompt-injection detection; non-blocking default | KL-3, KL-4; evaluator device is the planned fix path |
| T-agent-kill-switch | from_device self-report defeats halt; resume authorization unclear | Ticketed: T-halt-caller-auth-token |
| T-container-shim | Network isolation spec ambiguous; Docker socket; resource limits | Ticketed: T-container-shim-network-spec; KL-10 |
